text: Hello, my name is Kristen Bremer and I'm a general surgery resident at Creighton University School of Medicine in Omaha, Nebraska. Today I will be presenting the technical aspects of robotic excision of a large epiphrenic diverticulum. We have no conflicts of interest. An epiphrenic diverticulum is located in the distal esophagus above the diaphragm. These are typically caused by some type of esophageal motility disorder, which causes increased pressure on the wall of the esophagus, leading to diverticulum formation. They are false diverticula, more common on the right. Our patient is a fifty-six-year-old male who reported regurgitation for the past twelve years, which was worse with laying on his left side. These symptoms had worsened over the past two years, with worsening regurgitation and the addition of dysphagia and recurrent aspiration. The patient underwent barium swallow, which showed a large, wide-mouthed diverticulum, eleven point five centimeters in size and the greatest dimension. An EGD showed food and fluid in the esophagus with a wide-mouthed epiphrenic diverticulum and a tight lower esophageal sphincter. The second photo showed the sphincter on the left and the diverticulum on the right, indicated by the yellow arrow. The third photo is a view inside this diverticulum after the debris was cleared out with multiple passes of a Roth net. Manometry showed a normal lower esophageal sphincter mean IRP as well as distal esophageal spasm.

All options, including endoscopic management of the diverticulum, were discussed with the patient. The patient elected to undergo a robotic-assisted laparoscopic epiphrenic diverticulectomy with hellermyotomy and dora fundoplication.

The surgical team uses three 8mm robotic ports, a 12mm robotic port for the stapler, and an 8mm assist port in the right lower quadrant, along with a liver retractor. The initial approach involves mobilizing the right crura and entering the mediastinum, as shown in the video. The frenoesophageal membrane is fully mobilized, and the mediastinum and distal esophagus are mobilized up to 8 centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the diverticulum adherent to the pleura on the right side and performs a circumferential dissection starting from the distal aspect. The pleura is gently peeled away from the diverticulum, and posterior dissection is carried out. Endoscopy is used to outline the diverticulum relative to other structures, and blunt dissection techniques are employed to take down adhesions between the diverticulum and adjacent structures. The robotic-assisted camera provides stable visualization for mediastinal dissection, and adhesions on the proximal aspect of the diverticulum are removed.

The surgeon completes the separation of the diverticulum from surrounding structures and delivers it out of the mediastinum. The neck of the diverticulum is identified, extending from five to nine centimeters above the gastroesophageal junction. The diverticulectomy is performed using multiple endo-GI blue loads, with an endoscope used as a bougie. The stapled area is imbricated with vicryl sutures. A myotomy is carried out on the anterolateral wall, extending onto the stomach and above the gastroesophageal junction. The crura is approximated with interrupted silk sutures, followed by an anterior door fundoplication.
The intraoperative endoscopy reveals a well-functioning gastroesophageal junction with a visible fundoplication. The endoscopic view confirms the absence of any mucosal tears or mucostomy.

The divided diverticulum is shown being removed using an endobag. The post-operative course is visually uneventful, with the patient being discharged the following day after a barium swallow test showed no abnormalities.
